moveAnnotations.annotation: We open with e4, seizing the center and preparing for one of the most romantic openings in chess history. The Evans Gambit starts here -- with the King's Pawn controlling d5 and f5, opening lines for the bishop and queen. Every great attacking game from the 1800s to Kasparov's modern revivals begins with this move.
plans: Rapid development toward the Italian Game structure
Control the center and open lines for pieces
alternatives: d4 - Queen's Pawn, different character entirely

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black responds symmetrically, occupying the center and opening the diagonal for the f8 bishop. This leads to the Open Game where both sides get active piece play early. Black's e5 pawn mirrors White's central claim and sets up the tactical battles that define 1.e4 e5 openings.
alternatives: c5 - the Sicilian, the most popular response at top levels

moveAnnotations.annotation: Knight to f3, attacking e5 and developing toward the kingside. This is the most natural second move -- we threaten the e5 pawn, develop a piece, prepare to castle, and control the d4 square. Black must respond to the threat, which keeps the initiative on our side. Every Italian Game and Evans Gambit flows through this move.
plans: Bc4 next for the Italian setup
Prepare rapid kingside castling

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black defends e5 with the knight, the most natural and principled response. Nc6 develops a piece to its best square while protecting the center. The knight on c6 also influences d4 and e5, making it a cornerstone of Black's position. We're now in the most common e4 e5 starting structure.
alternatives: Nf6 - the Petrov, very solid but less combative

moveAnnotations.annotation: Bishop to c4, entering the Italian Game. The bishop aims at f7, Black's Achilles' heel that's only defended by the king. From c4, the bishop controls the critical d5 square and supports future central play. We need this bishop on this diagonal for the Evans Gambit to work -- without the pressure on f7 and the center, the pawn sacrifice wouldn't make sense.
plans: Set up the Evans Gambit with b4
Maintain pressure on the a2-f7 diagonal
alternatives: Bb5 - the Ruy Lopez, equally strong but different philosophy

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the bishop actively to c5, creating the Giuoco Piano position ('quiet game' -- though it's about to get very loud). The bishop on c5 mirrors White's pressure by eyeing f2 and controlling the a7-g1 diagonal. This is the position where Captain Evans, playing aboard a mail ship in 1827, invented the gambit we're about to play.
plans: Counter-pressure on f2 and the dark squares
Prepare d6 and solid development
alternatives: Nf6 - the Two Knights Defence, also sharp
Be7 - the Hungarian, more passive

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Evans Gambit! We sacrifice a whole pawn on b4 to lure Black's bishop away from the c5 square. Why? Because once that bishop moves, we're going to play c3 and d4, blasting open the center with a massive lead in development. This is one of the most principled gambits in chess -- we're not gambling, we're investing. A pawn for time, space, and a devastating initiative. Morphy, Kasparov, and Nakamura have all crushed opponents with this move.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White sacrifices the b-pawn to accelerate central play with c3 and d4.
plans: After Bxb4, play c3 to kick the bishop and prepare d4
Open the center while ahead in development

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black takes the pawn -- declining the Evans Gambit with moves like Bb6 is possible but generally gives White a comfortable position for free. By accepting, Black gets a material advantage but falls behind in development. The bishop on b4 is awkwardly placed and will be kicked around, giving White exactly the tempi needed to build a monster center.
alternatives: Bb6 - the Evans Gambit Declined, solid but passive
Ba5 - keeps the bishop active but White still gets c3-d4

moveAnnotations.annotation: Immediately kicking the bishop and preparing d4. This is the whole point of the Evans Gambit -- c3 attacks the bishop with tempo and sets up the d4 pawn push. After the bishop retreats, we'll play d4 with a huge center and rapid development. White's compensation for the pawn is already visible: we're about to have pawns on e4 and d4 while Black is scrambling to develop. The initiative is worth more than a pawn here.
plans: d4 next move to blow open the center
Develop all pieces rapidly while Black deals with the bishop
alternatives: O-O - castling first is a different gambit approach but c3 is sharper

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black retreats the bishop to a5, maintaining the pin on the c3 pawn (it's pinned to the king along the a5-e1 diagonal). This is considered the main line and the best retreat. The bishop stays active on the a5-e1 diagonal and keeps some pressure on White's queenside. Compared to Be7 or Bc5, Ba5 is the most combative choice -- Black isn't giving up the initiative without a fight.
plans: Maintain the pin on c3 to slow White's d4 plans
Develop quickly and try to consolidate the extra pawn
alternatives: Be7 - safe retreat but passive, gives White easy d4
Bc5 - returns to the original square but blocks c7 pawn

moveAnnotations.annotation: The center explodes! d4 is the culmination of the Evans Gambit -- we now have a massive pawn center on e4 and d4, attacking Black's e5 pawn. If Black takes on d4, we recapture with the bishop or open even more lines. The position is opening up and our pieces are going to pour into the center. This is what we sacrificed the pawn for -- look at all those open diagonals and the lead in development.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's e4-d4 center is dominant, attacking Black's e5 and opening lines for all pieces.
plans: Open the center and exploit the development advantage
Castle and bring the full army into the attack

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black takes on d4, which opens the center -- exactly what White wants in a gambit. But Black doesn't have a great alternative: if Black doesn't take, White plays dxe5 winning a pawn back with an even better position. So Black accepts that the center opens and tries to weather the storm. The challenge now is that White has open lines and rapid development, while Black still needs several moves to get organized.
alternatives: d6 - tries to hold the center but dxe5 dxe5, Qxd8+ is unpleasant

moveAnnotations.annotation: We castle into the attack! Rather than recapturing on d4 immediately, we get the king safe and bring the rook into play. The f1 rook will soon land on d1 or support the center. This is strong psychology too -- we're so confident in our position that we don't even bother taking the pawn back immediately. The threat of cxd4 is always there, and meanwhile we're fully developed while Black is still struggling.
plans: cxd4 when the time is right to establish a massive center
Develop with Ba3, Nbd2, or Qb3 to increase pressure
alternatives: cxd4 - recapturing immediately is also strong but O-O is more flexible

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays d6 to shore up the e5 square (which doesn't have a pawn anymore) and open a retreat for the bishop to b6. d6 is a solid, defensive move that tries to stabilize the position. Black's plan is simple: survive the initial onslaught, keep the extra pawn, and reach an endgame where material matters more than initiative. But White's lead in development means the middlegame comes first.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black's d6 pawn supports the center but creates a relatively passive structure.
plans: Stabilize and develop the remaining pieces
Keep the extra pawn and trade down to an endgame
alternatives: Nf6 - develops and attacks e4 but d6 is needed for the bishop retreat

moveAnnotations.annotation: Now we recapture, establishing the ideal pawn center: e4 and d4. This is a textbook dream center -- two pawns abreast controlling c5, d5, e5, and f5. Combined with our castled king and developed pieces, this center gives us enormous spatial advantage. Every piece has open lines, the bishop on c4 is a monster, and the d4 pawn controls key squares. The Evans Gambit has delivered exactly what it promised.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The e4-d4 center is the pinnacle of the Evans Gambit -- total central domination for the cost of one pawn.
plans: Develop remaining pieces and build a kingside attack
Use the center pawns to restrict Black's pieces

moveAnnotations.annotation: The bishop retreats to b6, where it's solid but no longer putting pressure on the c3-e1 diagonal. From b6 the bishop eyes d4 and f2, which provides some counterplay, but it's mostly a defensive retreat. Black is trying to complete development and get the king castled. The bishop on b6 is decent but compared to White's actively placed pieces, it's clear who has the initiative.
plans: Pressure d4 and the f2 point
Support d5 break in the future
alternatives: Be7 - very passive but solid
Bc7 - similar idea to b6 but more cramped

moveAnnotations.annotation: Knight to c3, developing the last minor piece and adding more firepower to the center. The knight controls d5 -- a dream outpost -- and supports the e4 pawn. From c3 it can jump to d5 at the perfect moment for a devastating blow. We now have every piece developed or near-developed (just the c1 bishop and rooks to activate), while Black is still struggling to castle. This development lead is the entire compensation for the Evans Gambit pawn.
plans: Nd5 at the right moment for a crushing blow
Bg5 or Ba3 to complete development and increase pressure
alternatives: Bg5 - pins the knight immediately but Nc3 is more flexible

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black finally develops the kingside knight to f6, attacking e4 and preparing to castle. This is long overdue -- Black needed this knight in the game several moves ago. Nf6 is the most natural and necessary move, defending against White's central dominance and getting one step closer to castling. But White still has a significant development advantage and the powerful center. The Evans Gambit middlegame is just beginning, and White's initiative is very real.
plans: Castle kingside urgently
Challenge e4 with the Nf6 and look for d5 counterplay
alternatives: Na5 - harasses the Bc4 but leaves the kingside undeveloped

moveAnnotations.annotation: White opens with the king's pawn, immediately claiming central space and opening diagonals for the bishop and queen. This move controls the crucial d5 and f5 squares, preventing Black from establishing pieces there. It's the most direct way to fight for the center and begins White's aggressive intentions in the Evans Gambit system.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White creates a classical pawn center presence, with the e4 pawn controlling key central squares and supporting future piece development.
plans: Develop pieces quickly toward the center
Prepare Nf3 to support the center and attack e5

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black mirrors White's central advance, claiming equal space in the center and opening lines for piece development. This pawn controls d6 and f6, preventing White's pieces from occupying these key squares. The symmetrical response leads to open, tactical positions where both sides will fight for piece activity.

White develops the knight to its most natural square, attacking the e5 pawn and preparing to castle kingside. This move supports White's central control while developing toward the center. The knight from f3 can later jump to powerful outposts like d4 or g5, and it clears the path for castling.
plans: Attack the e5 pawn to gain central pressure
Prepare kingside castling for safety

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black defends the e5 pawn with natural development, bringing the knight toward the center where it controls important squares. This knight supports Black's central pawn and prepares for further piece coordination. From c6, the knight eyes the d4 and b4 squares, potentially disrupting White's plans.

White develops the bishop to an aggressive square, targeting the f7 pawn - Black's weakest point in the opening. This move is the hallmark of the Italian Game family, putting immediate pressure on Black's kingside. The bishop controls the important central diagonal and prepares for potential tactics involving f7.
plans: Maintain pressure on f7
Support future d3-d4 pawn break

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black mirrors White's bishop development, placing it on the active long diagonal and targeting f2. This creates symmetrical tension and puts counter-pressure on White's kingside. The bishop supports Black's central control and prepares to meet White's aggressive intentions with equally active piece play.

The Evans Gambit begins! White sacrifices a pawn to deflect Black's active bishop and gain time for development. This bold move offers the b4 pawn to open lines and accelerate White's piece coordination. The gambit aims to create a powerful pawn center and dominate with active pieces.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White creates an unbalanced pawn structure, sacrificing material for development tempo and central control.
plans: Force Black's bishop to retreat and lose time
Build a strong pawn center with c3 and d4

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black accepts the gambit pawn, winning material but allowing White to gain tempo and space. Taking on b4 is the principled response - Black gets an extra pawn but must be prepared for White's aggressive follow-up. The bishop now occupies a somewhat awkward square and will need to find a better home.
alternatives: Bd6 declines the gambit, maintaining central control

moveAnnotations.annotation: White immediately attacks the bishop, forcing it to make a decision while preparing to build a powerful pawn center. This move supports the crucial d4 advance and gives White excellent compensation for the sacrificed pawn. The c3 pawn also opens the c-file for potential rook activity later.
plans: Force Black's bishop to an inferior square
Prepare d4 to dominate the center

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black retreats the bishop to a5, maintaining the extra pawn while keeping the bishop somewhat active. This is the most challenging defense, as the bishop stays on the board and can potentially return to c5 later. However, the bishop is now less centralized and Black must be careful about White's mounting pressure.
alternatives: Bc5 allows d4, giving White excellent compensation

moveAnnotations.annotation: White strikes in the center, opening lines and challenging Black's e5 pawn. This powerful advance gives White excellent piece coordination and opens the position to favor active development. The d4 pawn supported by c3 creates an ideal pawn center, and White's pieces are perfectly placed to exploit the opening lines.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White establishes a classical pawn center with pawns on c3, d4, and e4, controlling key central squares and opening diagonals.
plans: Open the center to activate pieces
Exploit superior development

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black defends the e5 pawn with a solid but passive move, avoiding immediate complications. This move supports the center but blocks the dark-squared bishop's development and leads to a cramped position. Black chooses solidity over activity, preparing to weather White's initiative.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black creates a solid but passive pawn structure, with d6 supporting e5 but limiting piece mobility.
alternatives: exd4 opens the center immediately but gives White excellent piece play

moveAnnotations.annotation: The key move of the Slow Variation! White brings the queen to b3, putting tremendous pressure on f7 and preparing to increase the attack. This move is more forcing than immediate castling because it creates concrete threats that Black must address. The queen eyes both f7 and the b7 pawn, creating multiple targets.
plans: Maintain pressure on f7 before castling
Prepare to intensify the attack with piece coordination

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black defends f7 with the queen, meeting White's threats head-on but placing the queen on a somewhat awkward square. This move prevents immediate tactics on f7 but the queen lacks good squares and may become a target. Black must now coordinate carefully to avoid falling behind in development.

White opens the center at the perfect moment, having maximized pressure with Qb3. This capture removes Black's central pawn and opens lines for White's pieces while Black's king remains uncastled. The timing is crucial - White has enough pressure to make this opening beneficial.
plans: Open lines for piece activity
Exploit Black's uncastled king position

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black recaptures, maintaining material balance but opening the d-file where White's pieces can become active. This recapture is practically forced, but it allows White's rook to potentially use the d-file and keeps Black's king in the center longer. The position remains dynamically balanced but White keeps the initiative.

Now White castles, having extracted maximum value from the queen's pressure on f7. With the center opened and Black's pieces somewhat uncoordinated, White's king finds safety while the rook joins the attack via the d-file. This perfectly timed castling maintains all of White's advantages while securing the king.
plans: Activate the rook on the d-file
Maintain piece pressure against Black's uncastled king

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black retreats the bishop to safety, preparing to castle and consolidate the position. The bishop finds a more secure square on b6 but remains somewhat out of play. This move shows Black's practical approach - securing the pieces and preparing to complete development rather than trying to hold onto every advantage.

White develops the bishop with tempo, targeting Black's uncastled king and controlling key light squares. This move prevents Black from castling kingside easily and maintains pressure on the eighth rank. The bishop is perfectly placed to support White's initiative and create long-term positional pressure.
plans: Prevent or complicate Black's kingside castling
Maintain pressure on light squares around Black's king

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black attacks the queen and bishop simultaneously, forcing White to make a decision while activating the knight. This aggressive move shows Black fighting for counterplay rather than passively defending. The knight on a5 attacks both b3 and c4, creating tactical opportunities and forcing White to calculate carefully.
alternatives: O-O-O attempts to castle, but White has strong pressure

moveAnnotations.annotation: White opens with the King's Pawn, immediately seizing central space and opening lines for the bishop and queen. This classical move controls the d5 and f5 squares while allowing rapid piece development. The e4 pawn serves as the foundation for White's aggressive central strategy in the Evans Gambit.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White establishes a central pawn presence, controlling key dark squares in the center and preparing to support it with pieces.
plans: Develop pieces quickly toward the kingside
Prepare to castle and launch an attack

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black responds symmetrically, claiming equal central space and controlling the d4 and f4 squares. This move opens the diagonal for Black's bishop and creates immediate central tension. The e5 pawn mirrors White's strategy while preparing for rapid kingside development.

White develops the kingside knight to its most natural square, immediately attacking Black's e5 pawn and controlling key central squares. This move supports the center while preparing kingside castling. The knight on f3 is perfectly placed to support White's upcoming aggressive piece play in the Evans Gambit.
plans: Castle kingside quickly
Support the center and prepare piece coordination

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black defends the e5 pawn with the most natural developing move, bringing the knight to an active central square. This knight controls important squares like d4 and b4 while supporting Black's central pawn. The move follows classical opening principles by developing with tempo and maintaining the central tension.

White deploys the light-squared bishop to its most aggressive square, targeting Black's vulnerable f7 pawn and controlling the important a2-g8 diagonal. This move prepares the Evans Gambit by developing with maximum activity. The bishop on c4 creates immediate threats and supports White's kingside attacking ambitions.
plans: Launch the Evans Gambit with b4
Target the f7 weakness

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black mirrors White's bishop development, placing the bishop on an active diagonal and targeting f2. This natural developing move controls key central squares and prepares castling. However, this bishop will soon become a target in the Evans Gambit, where White will drive it around to gain tempo and central control.

The Evans Gambit begins! White sacrifices a pawn to drive Black's bishop away and gain rapid development plus central control. This bold move forces Black to make a decision about the bishop while White prepares to build a powerful pawn center with c3 and d4. The gambit offers dynamic compensation through superior piece activity and attacking chances.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White creates an asymmetrical pawn structure, sacrificing material for rapid development and central dominance.
plans: Force Black's bishop to move and lose tempo
Build a strong pawn center with c3 and d4

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black accepts the Evans Gambit by capturing the b4 pawn, gaining material but allowing White to build a strong center. This capture forces the bishop to an awkward square where it will face further harassment. While materially ahead, Black must now deal with White's rapid development and central pressure.
alternatives: Ba5 declining the gambit keeps the position more solid but allows White easy development

moveAnnotations.annotation: White continues the gambit plan, attacking Black's bishop and preparing to build the ideal pawn center with d4. This move forces Black's bishop to retreat while opening the queen's path to b3. The c3 pawn also supports the crucial d4 advance, giving White excellent central control in exchange for the sacrificed pawn.
plans: Force the bishop to retreat to a worse square
Play d4 next to dominate the center

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black retreats the bishop to a5, maintaining it on an active diagonal but to a less secure square. This move keeps the bishop in play while avoiding the pin that would occur on a3. However, the bishop on a5 remains exposed to further attacks and limits Black's piece coordination.
alternatives: Bc5 allows Qb3 with strong pressure
Be7 is passive but safer

moveAnnotations.annotation: White establishes the powerful pawn center that justifies the gambit, immediately attacking Black's e5 pawn and opening lines for piece development. This central advance creates maximum space advantage and opens the position for White's pieces. The d4 pawn works perfectly with the c3 pawn to control the center and restrict Black's pieces.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White achieves the ideal gambit pawn center with pawns on c3, d4, and e4, controlling vast central territory.
plans: Continue rapid development
Open lines for piece activity

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black captures the d4 pawn, opening the e-file but allowing White's pieces to become even more active. This trade helps Black's material advantage but gives White excellent piece play and open lines. The capture also opens the e-file, which White can potentially use with the rook after castling.

White castles kingside, bringing the king to safety while activating the f1 rook on the open e-file. This natural developing move completes White's kingside development and prepares to increase pressure. With the king safe, White can focus entirely on the attack against Black's exposed pieces and underdeveloped position.
plans: Use the rook on the e-file for pressure
Coordinate all pieces for maximum activity

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black makes a critical decision to capture the second pawn, gaining significant material but falling dangerously behind in development. This move opens the b-file for White's potential use and allows White to develop with enormous tempo. The 'Compromised Defence' begins here, as Black's greed creates serious tactical vulnerabilities.
alternatives: d3 or other moves keep the material but block the position

moveAnnotations.annotation: White's queen springs into action with a devastating move, targeting both the undefended f7 pawn and preparing tactical threats. This move exemplifies the Evans Gambit's attacking philosophy - rapid piece development with concrete threats. The queen on b3 coordinates perfectly with the bishop on c4 to create maximum pressure on Black's vulnerable kingside.
plans: Target the weak f7 square
Coordinate with pieces for tactical strikes

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black desperately tries to defend f7 while developing the queen, but this move blocks the bishop's retreat to e7 and leaves Black's position critically uncoordinated. The queen on e7 does protect f7 but fails to address White's mounting pressure. Black's king remains in the center, making the position extremely dangerous.
alternatives: d6 is more solid but still problematic
Qf6 defends but blocks the knight's development

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight with tempo, attacking Black's displaced bishop while bringing another piece into the attack. This move completes White's development advantage and increases pressure on Black's uncastled king. The knight on c3 is perfectly centralized and ready to jump to more aggressive squares like d5 or b5.
plans: Continue attacking the exposed black pieces
Look for tactical opportunities against the uncastled king

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black finally develops the kingside knight, attacking White's e4 pawn and preparing to potentially castle kingside. However, this development comes too late as White has already built an overwhelming position. The knight on f6 does provide some defensive help but cannot solve Black's fundamental problems of poor piece coordination and an exposed king.

White opens with the king's pawn, immediately staking a claim to the central squares d5 and f5. This classical move develops quickly toward the center and opens lines for both the bishop on f1 and the queen. The move sets the foundation for rapid piece development and potential kingside attacking chances.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White creates a central pawn on e4, controlling the key central squares d5 and f5, while opening the diagonal for the light-squared bishop.
plans: Develop pieces rapidly toward the center
Prepare to castle kingside for safety

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black mirrors White's central advance, establishing an equal foothold in the center. This move controls the critical squares d4 and f4, preventing White from advancing further in the center immediately. It's the most principled response, leading to open, tactical games where both sides develop actively.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black creates symmetrical central tension with pawns on e4 and e5, establishing a balanced pawn center that will influence piece development.
plans: Develop knights before bishops
Contest White's control of the center

moveAnnotations.annotation: The knight develops to its most active square, attacking the e5 pawn while supporting White's central control. From f3, the knight eyes several key squares including e5, d4, and g5, and prepares for rapid kingside development. This move is more forcing than alternatives like Nc3, putting immediate pressure on Black's position.
plans: Attack the e5 pawn to force Black to defend
Prepare rapid kingside development and castling

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black defends the e5 pawn with the most natural developing move. The knight on c6 not only protects e5 but also eyes the important d4 and b4 squares, potentially supporting future central advances. This flexible piece can later jump to d4, e7, or a5 depending on the position's demands.
plans: Maintain the central pawn on e5
Look for opportunities to advance d7-d5 or jump to d4

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the light-squared bishop to its most aggressive diagonal, targeting the f7 square—often Black's weakest point in the opening. This move prepares for rapid kingside development and potential tactics involving the f7 pawn. The bishop also supports possible future advances in the center and connects well with castling kingside.
plans: Target the weak f7 square
Complete kingside development and castle

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the light-squared bishop symmetrically, targeting f2 and controlling the long diagonal. This natural developing move prepares castling while maintaining the balance in the center. The bishop on c5 also supports potential tactical ideas and can later retreat to various squares if attacked.
plans: Complete development and castle kingside
Maintain pressure on White's position

moveAnnotations.annotation: White offers the Evans Gambit! This aggressive pawn sacrifice aims to deflect Black's bishop from the strong c5 square, gaining time and space in return for the pawn. If accepted, White gets rapid development, central control, and attacking chances. The gambit embodies the romantic attacking style of 19th-century chess.
plans: Gain rapid development if Black accepts
Open lines for piece activity
alternatives: 0-0 for solid development
d3 for a quieter positional approach

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black declines the Evans Gambit by retreating the bishop to safety. This solid choice avoids the tactical complications that arise after accepting with Bxb4. While Black doesn't win material immediately, the position remains balanced and Black maintains a secure setup without falling behind in development.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The pawn structure remains symmetrical with both sides having pawns on e4/e5, but White has advanced the b-pawn to b4.
plans: Complete development safely
Maintain solid position without tactical risks
alternatives: Bxb4 accepting the gambit for material but facing complications

moveAnnotations.annotation: White continues to pressure Black's light-squared bishop, threatening to trap it with a5. This move also prepares to open the a-file for the rook and maintains the initiative despite Black declining the gambit. White is building a spatial advantage on the queenside while keeping development options flexible.
plans: Threaten a5 to further pressure the bishop
Potentially open the a-file for rook activity

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black prevents White's threatened a5 advance, securing the bishop on b6 for the time being. This solid move also prepares potential queenside expansion with b7-b5 and maintains the flexibility of Black's position. While it doesn't contribute directly to development, it's a necessary prophylactic move.
plans: Secure the bishop's position
Potentially prepare b7-b5 expansion

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the queenside knight to its natural square, supporting the central e4 pawn and preparing for further piece development. The knight on c3 eyes several important squares including d5 and b5, and can potentially jump to d5 later for an outpost. This move also prepares White to complete development efficiently.
plans: Support central control and prepare d2-d3
Look for opportunities to establish a knight on d5

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the kingside knight to its most active square, attacking White's e4 pawn while preparing to castle. This natural move puts pressure on White's center and opens tactical possibilities. The knight on f6 also supports potential d7-d5 breaks and can later reposition to g4, h5, or e8 as needed.
plans: Complete kingside development and castle
Potentially support d7-d5 central break

moveAnnotations.annotation: White supports the attacked e4 pawn with a solid central move that also opens the diagonal for the light-squared bishop. This move prepares Be3 to contest the long diagonal and maintains White's central structure. While less aggressive than some alternatives, it leads to a sound positional setup.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White establishes a solid pawn triangle with pawns on d3, e4, creating a stable central foundation that supports piece development.
plans: Develop the bishop to e3
Complete kingside development
alternatives: Bd5 for more aggressive play
0-0 to castle first

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black mirrors White's setup, supporting the e5 pawn and preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop. This solid move creates a symmetrical pawn structure and opens the c8-h3 diagonal for the bishop. Black maintains equality and keeps the position balanced while preparing to complete development.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Both sides now have symmetrical pawn formations with central pawns supported by d-pawns, creating a stable but somewhat blocked center.
plans: Develop the dark-squared bishop
Complete kingside development and castle

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the dark-squared bishop to an active square, contesting Black's light-squared bishop on the long diagonal. This move also supports potential central advances and prepares castling. The bishop on e3 puts pressure on Black's position and can later support tactical ideas involving the center.
plans: Contest the long diagonal
Complete development with castling

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles kingside, bringing the king to safety while connecting the rooks. This natural developing move completes the basic kingside development and prepares for the middlegame. With the king secure, Black can focus on piece coordination and central play without worrying about tactical threats against the king.
plans: Connect the rooks and improve piece coordination
Look for central counterplay

moveAnnotations.annotation: White also castles kingside, securing the king's safety and bringing the rook to f1 where it supports the f-file. This completes White's basic development and prepares for middlegame operations. The position is roughly balanced, but White has slight pressure due to the bishop pair and spatial advantage.
plans: Utilize the bishop pair advantage
Create pressure along the f-file

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black exchanges bishops, eliminating White's dark-squared bishop and reducing White's attacking potential. This simplifying move eases Black's position by reducing the number of pieces that can create tactical threats. However, it also helps White by opening the f-file for the rook and clearing the way for potential central advances.
plans: Simplify the position
Reduce White's tactical possibilities

moveAnnotations.annotation: White recaptures with the f-pawn, opening the f-file for the rook and creating a strong pawn center. This capture gives White excellent practical chances with pressure down the f-file, particularly targeting f7. The doubled e-pawns actually strengthen White's central control and provide excellent support for piece play.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White now has doubled e-pawns but gains an open f-file and strong central control. The pawn structure favors White's pieces and creates attacking chances.
plans: Utilize the open f-file for attacking chances
Use the strong pawn center for piece coordination

moveAnnotations.annotation: White opens with the king's pawn, immediately claiming central space and preparing rapid piece development. This move controls the crucial d5 and f5 squares while opening diagonals for both the bishop and queen. The e4 move is the foundation of countless tactical openings, including the Evans Gambit we're about to see.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White establishes a central pawn on e4, creating the classic King's Pawn opening structure that emphasizes quick development and central control.
plans: Develop the kingside pieces quickly with Nf3 and Bc4
Castle early to ensure king safety
alternatives: d4 leads to Queen's Pawn openings with different strategic themes

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black responds symmetrically, meeting White's central advance with an equal claim to the center. This move controls d6 and f6 while opening lines for Black's own pieces. The e5 pawn creates immediate tension in the center and leads to the open, tactical games that characterize the King's Pawn openings.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The classic double king pawn opening emerges, with both sides controlling key central squares and creating a symmetrical pawn tension that will likely be resolved through piece play.
plans: Develop knights to their natural squares
Prepare to castle kingside
alternatives: Sicilian Defense with c5 creates unbalanced pawn structures

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to its most natural square, immediately attacking Black's e5 pawn and preparing rapid kingside development. From f3, the knight controls key central squares and supports future tactical operations. This move is virtually mandatory in King's Pawn openings, as it combines development with concrete threats.
plans: Attack the e5 pawn to force Black's response
Prepare Bc4 to complete rapid kingside development
alternatives: f4 (King's Gambit) sacrifices material for quick attack but is less sound

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black defends the e5 pawn with the most natural developing move, bringing the knight to an active central square. From c6, the knight not only protects e5 but also prepares ...Nf6 or controls important squares like d4. This is the most principled response, maintaining the central tension while developing harmoniously.
plans: Complete kingside development with ...Nf6
Consider ...f5 to support the e5 pawn later
alternatives: d6 defends passively but blocks the bishop
f5 is premature and weakening

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the bishop to its most aggressive square, targeting the f7 pawn—often Black's weakest point in the opening. The Italian Bishop setup on c4 prepares rapid castling while creating immediate tactical threats. This bishop placement is characteristic of sharp, tactical openings and sets the stage for the Evans Gambit sacrifice.
plans: Castle kingside on the next few moves
Prepare the b4 gambit sacrifice to disrupt Black's development
alternatives: Bb5 (Spanish Opening) focuses on piece pressure rather than direct attack

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black mirrors White's bishop development, creating the Italian Game position where both bishops target the opponent's kingside. The c5 bishop is actively placed, controlling important central squares and keeping tactical possibilities alive. This symmetrical development maintains the balance while preparing ...f5 or other aggressive continuations.
plans: Complete development with ...Nf6
Consider ...f5 to create kingside space
alternatives: Be7 is safer but less ambitious
f5 immediately creates complications but may be premature

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Evans Gambit begins! White sacrifices a pawn to disrupt Black's bishop and gain crucial tempo for rapid development. This aggressive pawn sacrifice is the hallmark of the Evans Gambit—White offers material to open lines and accelerate the attack. The b4 move forces Black's bishop to make a decision, and any retreat will give White a significant lead in development.
plans: Follow up with c3 to support the center after Black captures
Rapid development to exploit the time gained
alternatives: d3 leads to quieter Italian Game variations
0-0 is solid but less forcing

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black accepts the gambit, capturing the pawn but allowing White to gain the initiative through rapid development. Taking on b4 is the most principled response—declining the gambit with ...Bd6 or ...Be7 gives White a strong center without compensation. However, Black must now be very careful about piece placement as White's development advantage can quickly become dangerous.
plans: Find a safe square for the bishop
Complete kingside development carefully
alternatives: Bd6 declines the gambit but allows d4 with strong center
Be7 is too passive

moveAnnotations.annotation: White continues the gambit plan, supporting the center and preparing d4 with maximum force. The c3 move not only prepares central expansion but also attacks the bishop on b4, forcing it to make another move. This is the key follow-up to the Evans Gambit—White sacrifices time to move the same pawn twice because the resulting central control and development advantage justifies the investment.
plans: Play d4 on the next move to dominate the center
Continue rapid development to exploit Black's awkward pieces
alternatives: a3 immediately attacks the bishop but is less forcing than c3

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black chooses the Stone-Ware Variation, retreating the bishop to a more defensive but solid square. Unlike the main line ...Ba5, this retreat immediately prepares ...f5 and maintains better piece coordination. The bishop on d6 is somewhat less active than on a5, but it's also safer from future attacks and doesn't block the a-pawn's advance.
plans: Consider ...f5 to challenge White's center
Complete development with ...Nge7 or ...Nf6
alternatives: Ba5 is the main line, keeping the bishop more active
Bc5 allows d4 with tempo

moveAnnotations.annotation: White strikes in the center with maximum force, opening lines and creating immediate tactical threats. The d4 advance is the point of the entire gambit sequence—White has sacrificed a pawn and accepted doubled c-pawns to achieve this powerful central breakthrough. This move attacks the e5 pawn and opens the position for White's better-developed pieces.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White now has a classical pawn center with pawns on c3, d4, and e4, controlling key central squares and providing excellent piece coordination possibilities.
plans: Follow up by capturing on e5 to open more lines
Continue rapid development to exploit the initiative
alternatives: 0-0 is safer but less forcing than the immediate central strike

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight while defending the e4 square and preparing to challenge White's aggressive setup. This natural developing move puts pressure on White's e4 pawn and prepares ...0-0. The knight on f6 is well-placed to support Black's central defense, though Black must still be careful about White's growing initiative and superior piece activity.
plans: Castle kingside for safety
Consider ...Bg4 to pin the f3 knight
alternatives: exd4 opens the center immediately but may favor White's development
f5 is aggressive but weakening

moveAnnotations.annotation: White captures on e5, opening the center and maintaining the initiative despite being a pawn down. This exchange removes Black's central strongpoint and opens lines for White's pieces, particularly the queen and remaining bishop. The capture also prepares to drive away the bishop from d6, further disrupting Black's coordination.
plans: Attack the bishop on d6 with the next move
Continue development to exploit the open position
alternatives: Castling is solid but allows Black to consolidate

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black recaptures with the bishop, centralizing it on an excellent square where it controls many important squares. From e5, the bishop is very actively placed, putting pressure on White's position and maintaining material balance. However, this bishop can become a target, and Black must be prepared for White's continued aggressive play.
plans: Consolidate the position and complete development
Look for opportunities to exchange pieces to ease the pressure
alternatives: Nxe5 also recaptures but leaves the bishop on d6 somewhat awkward

moveAnnotations.annotation: White captures the bishop with the knight, winning material and maintaining a strong initiative. This tactical blow is the payoff for White's gambit play—by forcing favorable exchanges, White has effectively regained the sacrificed pawn with interest. The knight on e5 is powerfully centralized and creates multiple threats against Black's position.
plans: Maintain the knight on e5 or exchange favorably
Continue development to press the advantage
alternatives: Other moves allow Black to consolidate the extra material

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black recaptures with the knight, maintaining material balance but allowing White to keep the initiative. The knight on e5 is actively placed, but Black's pieces remain somewhat uncoordinated compared to White's harmonious development. Black must now focus on consolidating the position and completing development safely.
plans: Complete development and castle for safety
Look to exchange pieces to reduce White's attacking potential
alternatives: Other recaptures are inferior as they don't develop

moveAnnotations.annotation: White retreats the bishop to safety while maintaining pressure on Black's position. From b3, the bishop continues to eye the f7 square and stays out of any potential ...d6-d5 advances. This move maintains White's excellent piece coordination and keeps all pieces actively placed for potential tactical opportunities.
plans: Complete development with castling
Maintain pressure and look for tactical opportunities
alternatives: Bxf7+ is a premature sacrifice that doesn't work here

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black solidifies the position with a useful pawn move that supports the knight on e5 and prepares further development. The d6 advance creates a solid pawn structure and opens a path for the light-squared bishop to develop. While this move is somewhat slow, it addresses Black's main concern of piece coordination and provides a stable foundation for the middlegame.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black creates a solid pawn chain with pawns on d6, f7, g7, and h7, providing good king safety and supporting the centralized knight.
plans: Complete development with ...Be6 or ...Bg4
Castle kingside to ensure king safety
alternatives: 0-0 immediately is more direct
c6 prepares ...d5 but is less flexible

moveAnnotations.annotation: White opens with the king's pawn, immediately claiming the central e4 square and preparing rapid piece development. This move controls the d5 and f5 squares, preventing Black from establishing pieces there early. The e4 pawn also opens diagonal lines for both the bishop on f1 and the queen, setting the stage for aggressive play that characterizes the Evans Gambit system.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Classical center with White controlling e4 and preparing to contest the center with d2-d4 later.
plans: Develop pieces rapidly toward the center and kingside
Prepare d2-d4 to establish a strong pawn center

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black responds symmetrically, claiming an equal share of the center and mirroring White's central control. This move controls d6 and f6, preventing White's pieces from occupying these key squares. The e5 pawn also opens the f8-bishop's diagonal and prepares natural piece development, leading to the Italian Game complex after the expected knight developments.

White develops the knight to its most natural square, attacking Black's e5 pawn and controlling key central squares. This move is essential preparation for the Italian Game setup, as the knight supports potential d2-d4 advances and guards against Black's central expansion. From f3, the knight also prepares to support a kingside attack or central operations depending on how the position develops.
plans: Attack the e5 pawn to gain central pressure
Prepare Bc4 to complete the Italian Game setup

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black defends the e5 pawn with natural development, placing the knight on its most active square. This move not only secures the central pawn but also prepares to contest the d4 square and supports potential counterplay. The knight on c6 is perfectly placed for the coming Italian Game positions, where it can later support ...d7-d6 or jump to more active squares like a5 or e7.

White completes the Italian Game setup by developing the bishop to its most aggressive square, targeting the f7 weakness and controlling the central d5 square. This bishop placement is crucial for the Evans Gambit, as it will remain on the long diagonal to maintain pressure even after Black's bishop retreats. The bishop also supports potential tactics involving the f7 square and prepares for the gambit sacrifice on b4.
plans: Target the f7 square, Black's weakest point
Prepare the Evans Gambit with b2-b4

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black mirrors White's development, placing the bishop on its most active square where it eyes the f2 weakness and controls central squares. This natural developing move completes Black's kingside piece coordination and prepares castling. However, this bishop will soon become a target for White's Evans Gambit pawn sacrifice, which aims to disrupt Black's smooth development and gain time for a powerful central advance.

The Evans Gambit begins! White sacrifices a pawn to disrupt Black's bishop and gain crucial time for central development. This bold pawn sacrifice aims to drive the bishop away from its strong c5 outpost, allowing White to build a powerful pawn center with c3 and d4. The gambit embodies the Romantic era chess philosophy of rapid development and attacking chances over material considerations.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White creates an asymmetrical structure, offering the b4 pawn for developmental advantages and central control.
plans: Force Black's bishop to retreat and lose time
Build a strong pawn center with c3 and d4
alternatives: 3...Be7 leads to the Hungarian Defense, avoiding the Evans Gambit complications

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black accepts the gambit pawn, taking the material but allowing White to gain time and space for piece development. This capture is the most principled response, as declining the gambit allows White to maintain the initiative without compensation. However, Black must now be prepared to face White's aggressive central advance and rapid piece mobilization, making precise defensive moves essential.
plans: Find a safe retreat square for the bishop
Complete development while maintaining the extra pawn

moveAnnotations.annotation: White immediately attacks the bishop, forcing it to make a decision while preparing the crucial d2-d4 advance. This move is the key to the Evans Gambit's strategy - by gaining time against the bishop, White can quickly establish a dominant pawn center. The c3 pawn also controls important squares like b4 and d4, supporting White's central expansion and preventing Black from easily consolidating the extra pawn.
plans: Force the bishop retreat to gain more time
Launch d2-d4 to dominate the center

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black retreats the bishop to a5, maintaining its influence on the center while staying out of immediate danger. This square allows the bishop to potentially retreat to b6 if further attacked, and it keeps pressure on central squares. However, the bishop is somewhat sidelined on a5, and Black must be careful about potential tactics involving the knight on c6 and the weakened b7 square.
alternatives: 10...Bc5 keeps the bishop more centralized but allows White strong pressure after d4

moveAnnotations.annotation: White achieves the key breakthrough, establishing a powerful pawn center and opening lines for piece development. This move attacks the e5 pawn, forces Black to make critical decisions about the center, and opens the c1-bishop's diagonal. The d4 advance represents the culmination of the Evans Gambit strategy - White has gained time, space, and initiative in exchange for the sacrificed pawn, creating the complex tactical positions that characterize this opening.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White establishes a classical pawn center with pawns on e4 and d4, controlling key central squares and providing excellent piece coordination.
plans: Open the center to exploit the lead in development
Activate all pieces rapidly for a kingside attack

moveAnnotations.annotation: Lasker's Defense begins with this solid pawn move that supports the e5 pawn and prepares to return the gambit material for safer development. This move represents a more positional approach than the sharp tactical lines, aiming to consolidate Black's position and complete development before facing White's initiative. The d6 pawn also opens the c8-bishop's diagonal and prepares potential counterplay in the center.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black creates a solid pawn chain with pawns on e5 and d6, providing mutual support and controlling key central squares.
plans: Return the extra pawn to complete development safely
Prepare ...Bb6 and castle kingside
alternatives: ...exd4 leads to sharper tactical lines where Black tries to hold the extra material

moveAnnotations.annotation: White castles to safety while maintaining maximum pressure on Black's position. This move brings the rook to f1, supporting potential f2-f4 advances and keeping the king safe from potential counterattacks. By castling quickly, White maintains the initiative and can continue developing pieces aggressively without worrying about king safety, which is crucial in the tactical positions arising from the Evans Gambit.
plans: Bring the rook to f1 to support central operations
Prepare f2-f4 to increase central pressure

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black repositions the bishop to a more secure and active square, where it cannot be easily attacked and maintains influence over central squares. This move is a key part of Lasker's defensive setup, improving the bishop's position while preparing to castle. The bishop on b6 also eyes the f2 square and supports potential tactics along the a7-g1 diagonal, giving Black some counterplay possibilities.

White opens the center by capturing the e5 pawn, creating immediate tactical complications and opening lines for piece activity. This exchange transforms the position's character, as White gains central space and opens the d-file for the queen and potential rook activity. The capture also threatens to win material if Black recaptures carelessly, forcing Black to respond precisely to maintain equality.
plans: Open lines for maximum piece activity
Exploit the lead in development with tactical threats

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black recaptures, maintaining material balance and keeping the central pawn structure relatively stable. This recapture returns the gambit pawn, fulfilling Lasker's defensive strategy of achieving a solid position without material deficit. However, Black must now deal with White's active pieces and potential threats, particularly the exposed king position that still needs to castle.

White activates the queen aggressively, targeting the b7 and f7 squares while putting pressure on Black's queenside. This move creates multiple threats and forces Black to be extremely careful about piece coordination and king safety. The queen on b3 also eyes potential tactics involving the bishop on b6 and creates uncomfortable pin possibilities along the b-file if Black castles queenside.
plans: Target weak squares on b7 and f7
Create tactical complications before Black can castle

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black brings the queen into active play, defending the f7 square and preparing potential counterplay against White's kingside. This move addresses White's immediate threats while creating counter-threats of her own, particularly against the f2 and f3 squares. The queen on f6 also supports potential piece exchanges that could ease Black's defensive burden and transition to a more manageable endgame.
plans: Defend f7 and create counterplay on the kingside
Prepare to exchange pieces to reduce White's attacking potential

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the bishop with tempo, attacking Black's queen and forcing it to make a decision while increasing pressure on Black's kingside. This move exemplifies perfect piece coordination in the Evans Gambit - every piece is actively placed and contributing to the attack. The bishop on g5 also pins potential knight development to f6 and creates additional tactical motifs involving the h6 and f6 squares.
plans: Force the queen to move and gain more time
Increase kingside pressure with all pieces coordinated

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black retreats the queen to g6, maintaining defense of key squares while avoiding further harassment from White's pieces. This square allows the queen to stay active, defending both the kingside and potentially supporting counterplay. However, the queen on g6 is somewhat exposed and must be careful about potential discoveries or tactics involving White's well-coordinated pieces, particularly the bishop pair.

White centralizes the bishop to its most dominant square, controlling key central territory and putting maximum pressure on Black's position. This move threatens multiple squares and creates tactical complications that Black must navigate carefully. The bishop on d5 is perfectly placed, controlling both sides of the board and supporting potential tactics, while also preparing to potentially exchange on c6 to damage Black's pawn structure.
plans: Dominate the center with the powerful bishop
Consider Bxc6+ to damage Black's pawn structure
alternatives: ...Nge7 and ...O-O allows Black to complete development with a solid position

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black completes the Lasker Defense setup by developing the knight to e7, where it defends against White's central pressure and prepares to castle kingside. This move blocks the bishop's attack on f7 and prepares to challenge White's dominant bishop on d5. The knight on e7 is well-placed for defense and can later jump to more active squares like c6 or f5, giving Black good prospects for equalizing the position through solid development.
plans: Complete development with ...O-O
Challenge White's central bishop with ...Nxd5 if appropriate

moveAnnotations.annotation: King's pawn opening.
Black responds with e5.
Nf3 develops and attacks e5.
Nc6 defends.
Bc4 targets f7.
Bc5 mirrors, targeting f2.
b4! The Evans Gambit, sacrificing a pawn for rapid development.
Bxb4 accepts the gambit.
c3 kicks the bishop and prepares a massive pawn center.
Ba5 retreats, keeping the bishop active.
d4 opens the center with a powerful pawn duo.
exd4 captures.
Castles, getting the king safe and the rook active immediately.
Nge7 develops but is somewhat passive.
cxd4 recaptures, establishing a dominant pawn center.
d5 challenges the center.
exd5 opens lines for all of White's pieces.
Nxd5 recaptures centrally.
Ba3! Prevents castling by controlling the dark squares.
Be6 develops and blocks the e-file.
Bb5! Pins the knight on c6, creating multiple threats. White has a development avalanche with all pieces active while Black's king is stuck in the center.
King's pawn opening.
Black plays e5.
Nf3 develops.
Nc6 defends.
Bc4 targets f7.
Bc5 develops actively.
b4! The Evans Gambit.
Bxb4 accepts.
c3 pushes the bishop back.
Ba5 keeps the bishop on an active diagonal.
d4 establishes central dominance.
d6 tries to hold the center solidly instead of taking.
Qb3! Attacks f7 and b7, putting immediate pressure on Black.
Qd7 defends both targets but the queen is passively placed.
dxe5 opens the center favorably.
Bb6 retreats the bishop.
exd6 captures, creating a passed pawn.
Na5 attacks the queen, trying to win a tempo.
Qa4! Pins the knight to the queen, a nasty tactical shot.
Nxc4 grabs the bishop, but White has the d6 passer and a huge lead in development. The slow Evans build-up has created lasting pressure.
King's pawn opening.
Black responds with e5.
Nf3 develops.
Nc6 defends.
Bc4, the Italian bishop.
Bc5 develops.
b4! Evans Gambit.
Bxb4 accepts.
c3 gains a tempo.
Ba5 retreats.
d4 pushes for the center.
exd4 takes.
Castles with rapid development.
d3! A tricky defensive resource, blocking the d-file and keeping the extra pawn.
Qb3 pressures f7 and tries to open lines.
Qf6 defends f7 and eyes the kingside.
e5 pushes forward aggressively.
Qg6 avoids the pawn and targets g2.
Re1 supports the e5 pawn.
Nge7 develops behind the pawn wall.
Bg5 tries to create pressure, but Black is solid.
Castles safely. Black has weathered the Evans Gambit storm with the extra pawn and a solid position. The d3 blockade successfully neutralized White's initiative.
King's pawn opening.
Black plays e5.
Nf3 develops.
Nc6 defends.
Bc4 targets f7.
Bc5 mirrors.
b4! The Evans Gambit offered.
Bxb4 accepts.
c3 kicks the bishop.
Be7! A conservative retreat, declining to keep the bishop active. This solid approach avoids White's prepared attacking lines.
d4 pushes for central control.
Na5 attacks the bishop immediately.
Nxe5 grabs the center pawn.
Nxc4 wins the bishop pair.
Nxc4 recaptures.
d5 strikes at the center.
exd5 opens lines.
Qxd5 centralizes the queen.
Ne3 attacks the queen with development.
Qd8 retreats safely. Black has simplified to a near-equal position with the bishop pair and no weaknesses. The Evans Declined takes the sting out of White's gambit.
